_meta.authoringNotes: Definitions and classification per IASP central neuropathic pain taxonomy and Bowsher D. classic Dejerine-Roussy descriptions. Central post-stroke pain epidemiology per Klit H, Finnerup NB, Jensen TS. Lancet Neurol 2009 (PMID 19608100). Treatment evidence per Finnerup NB et al. Lancet Neurol 2015 systematic review and meta-analysis (PMID 25575710) and the NeuPSIG recommendations. Spinal cord injury pain per Siddall PJ et al. Pain 2003 (PMID 12855316). MS-related central pain per Truini A et al. Nat Rev Neurol 2013. Multidisciplinary management per IASP and British Pain Society guidance.

h1Title: Central Pain Syndrome: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Central pain syndrome is a chronic neuropathic pain disorder caused by damage to or dysfunction of the central nervous system itself — the brain, brainstem, or spinal cord — rather than peripheral nerves or tissues. It develops after stroke (central post-stroke pain, historically the Dejerine-Roussy thalamic syndrome), spinal cord injury, multiple sclerosis, traumatic brain injury, tumors, or syringomyelia. Central post-stroke pain affects roughly 8-35% of stroke survivors depending on lesion location, and chronic pain affects up to two-thirds of people with spinal cord injury. The pain is typically constant, burning, aching, or lancinating, often accompanied by allodynia (pain from normally non-painful stimuli such as light touch or cool air) and hyperalgesia, and it characteristically affects the body region corresponding to the central lesion. Onset can be immediate or delayed by weeks to months after the causative injury. Central pain is among the most treatment-resistant pain syndromes; first-line therapy uses antidepressants (amitriptyline, duloxetine) and anticonvulsants (pregabalin, gabapentin), with multidisciplinary pain management, physical rehabilitation, and psychological support essential because complete pain relief is uncommon.

keyStats.prevalence: Central post-stroke pain affects 8-35% of stroke survivors; chronic pain affects up to 65% of people with spinal cord injury (Siddall Pain 2003)
keyStats.demographics: Reflects the demographics of the underlying cause — older adults for stroke, younger adults for traumatic spinal cord injury and MS
keyStats.avgAge: Variable by cause; central post-stroke pain peaks over age 60, spinal cord injury pain in young adults
keyStats.globalCases: Several million people worldwide; the single largest contributor is the global stroke survivor population

definition: Central pain syndrome (ICD-10: G89.0 central pain syndrome; also coded under the underlying disorder) is chronic neuropathic pain arising directly from a lesion or disease of the central somatosensory nervous system — the spinothalamocortical pathways within the spinal cord, brainstem, thalamus, or cortex. It is distinct from nociceptive pain (from tissue injury) and from peripheral neuropathic pain (from damage to peripheral nerves). The defining mechanism is maladaptive reorganization and disinhibition within central pain pathways: loss of normal inhibitory control, central sensitization, altered thalamic firing, and cortical remapping generate spontaneous and evoked pain in the absence of ongoing peripheral input. The classic form is central post-stroke pain (Dejerine-Roussy syndrome when the lesion involves the thalamus), but central pain also follows spinal cord injury, multiple sclerosis demyelinating plaques, traumatic brain injury, intramedullary or thalamic tumors, syringomyelia, and Parkinson disease. Pain distribution maps to the body region represented by the damaged central structure — for example, hemibody pain after a thalamic stroke, or below-level pain after a spinal cord injury. The condition is managed by neurology, pain medicine, rehabilitation medicine, and psychiatry, ideally within a multidisciplinary chronic pain service.

typesClassification.description: Develops in 8-35% of stroke survivors, classically after lateral medullary or thalamic infarction. Hemibody burning or aching pain with allodynia, often delayed weeks to months after the stroke. The thalamic form is the historical Dejerine-Roussy syndrome.
At-level and below-level neuropathic pain affecting up to two-thirds of people with spinal cord injury. Below-level pain is diffuse, burning, and often most severe in completely insensate regions.
Demyelinating lesions in spinothalamic pathways produce continuous central neuropathic pain; trigeminal neuralgia and Lhermitte phenomenon are related MS pain syndromes.

typesClassification.type: Central pain from brain or spinal cord tumor, syringomyelia, or traumatic brain injury
typesClassification.description: Less common causes; pain distribution and severity depend on the location and extent of the central lesion.

A subset of Parkinson disease patients develop central neuropathic pain attributed to altered basal ganglia and thalamic processing, distinct from musculoskeletal and dystonic pain in the same population.

primarySymptoms: Constant or near-constant spontaneous pain described as burning, aching, scalding, or pressing, affecting the body region corresponding to the central lesion (for example one half of the body after a thalamic stroke).
Intermittent shooting, lancinating, or electric-shock-like pains superimposed on the constant background pain.
Allodynia — pain provoked by normally non-painful stimuli such as light touch, clothing, a breeze, or a change in temperature.
Hyperalgesia — exaggerated pain response to mildly painful stimuli.
Pain that is worsened by cold, emotional stress, fatigue, movement, or even loud noise and bright light in some patients.
Dysesthesia — unpleasant abnormal sensations such as crawling, tingling, or numbness coexisting with the pain.
Pain that is poorly localized, deep, and difficult for the patient to describe, often affecting an area that is also numb to formal sensory testing.
Delayed onset — pain frequently begins weeks to months after the causative stroke or injury rather than immediately.
Associated sleep disturbance, fatigue, low mood, and reduced participation in rehabilitation because of the pain.
Temperature-perception abnormalities in the painful region (impaired warm and cold discrimination) reflecting spinothalamic tract damage.
earlyWarningSigns: New burning or aching discomfort developing in a limb or body region weeks after a stroke or spinal cord injury
Increasing sensitivity to light touch or temperature change over a previously numb area
Difficulty tolerating bedsheets, clothing, or bathwater on the affected side
Disturbed sleep and reduced engagement in rehabilitation attributed to unexplained pain
Shooting pains superimposed on a constant background ache in a region matching a known central lesion
emergencySigns: Sudden worsening of pain with new neurological deficit — exclude recurrent stroke, cord compression, or expanding syrinx
Severe depression with suicidal ideation related to intractable pain — requires urgent mental health assessment
New bowel or bladder dysfunction with worsening pain in a spinal cord injury patient — exclude syringomyelia or cord tethering
Fever, focal weakness, and worsening pain — exclude spinal or intracranial infection
Acute severe drug toxicity (sedation, confusion, respiratory depression) from escalating opioid or adjuvant doses

causes.description: Infarction or hemorrhage affecting the thalamus, lateral medulla (Wallenberg syndrome), brainstem, or parietal cortex disrupts central pain processing. Lateral thalamic and lateral medullary lesions carry the highest risk of central post-stroke pain.

Traumatic or non-traumatic spinal cord injury damages ascending sensory pathways, producing at-level and below-level central neuropathic pain. Incomplete injuries and injuries with preserved partial sensation can be particularly painful.
Plaques within the spinal cord and brainstem spinothalamic pathways disrupt central pain modulation, producing continuous central neuropathic pain and related syndromes such as trigeminal neuralgia.

causes.cause: Brain or spinal cord tumor and syringomyelia
causes.description: Intramedullary spinal tumors, thalamic tumors, and a syrinx (fluid-filled cavity within the spinal cord) damage central sensory structures and generate central pain, sometimes as the presenting symptom.
Diffuse axonal injury and focal contusions affecting central sensory pathways can produce central neuropathic pain, often overlapping with post-traumatic headache and musculoskeletal pain.

Across all causes, the common final pathway is loss of inhibitory control, abnormal thalamic bursting activity, cortical reorganization, and central sensitization that perpetuate pain independent of any ongoing peripheral stimulus.

riskFactors.description: Lateral medullary (Wallenberg) and thalamic strokes carry the highest risk; central post-stroke pain develops in 8-35% of survivors depending on lesion location.
Up to two-thirds of people with spinal cord injury develop chronic pain, much of it central neuropathic in type.
Central neuropathic pain is one of the most common pain syndromes in MS, related to spinal and brainstem demyelination.
Some studies suggest central post-stroke pain is more frequent and more severe in younger stroke survivors.
Mood disorders amplify pain perception and reduce treatment response; identifying and treating them improves outcomes.

riskFactors.factor: Greater sensory deficit and allodynia early after injury
riskFactors.description: Marked spinothalamic sensory loss and the early presence of allodynia predict development of persistent central pain.
Poor sleep and inactivity worsen central pain; addressing them is part of effective management.

affectedDemographics: Central post-stroke pain: follows the demographics of stroke — most common over age 60, but more frequent and severe when stroke occurs in younger adults.
Spinal cord injury central pain: disproportionately affects young men, reflecting the epidemiology of traumatic spinal cord injury.
Multiple sclerosis central pain: affects the MS population, with female predominance and onset typically between ages 20 and 50.
No strong ethnic predilection beyond that of the underlying causative conditions.
Globally, the stroke survivor population is the single largest contributor to central pain syndrome prevalence.
diagnosisOverview: Central pain syndrome is a clinical diagnosis based on the combination of chronic neuropathic-type pain, a documented or strongly suspected central nervous system lesion, and a pain distribution that is neuroanatomically consistent with that lesion. The first step is a detailed pain history — quality (burning, aching, lancinating), distribution, time course relative to the causative event, and provoking and relieving factors — followed by a focused neurological examination. Sensory testing typically shows impaired spinothalamic modality function (reduced pain and temperature perception) in the painful region, often with coexisting allodynia and hyperalgesia; this combination of sensory loss plus pain in the same territory is characteristic. Validated screening tools such as the Douleur Neuropathique 4 (DN4), painDETECT, and the Leeds Assessment of Neuropathic Symptoms and Signs (LANSS) support a neuropathic mechanism. Brain or spinal cord MRI confirms the central lesion (thalamic or brainstem stroke, MS plaque, syrinx, tumor) and excludes new or progressive pathology. Quantitative sensory testing can document the spinothalamic deficit objectively. The diagnosis requires excluding peripheral neuropathic pain, nociceptive musculoskeletal pain (especially shoulder pain and spasticity-related pain after stroke or spinal cord injury, which are common confounders), complex regional pain syndrome, and pain from a peripheral nerve injury. The IASP grading system for neuropathic pain (possible, probable, definite) is applied: definite central pain requires a confirmatory central lesion plus a neuroanatomically plausible pain distribution and sensory signs.

diagnosticTests.test: Detailed neurological examination with sensory mapping
diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents the pattern of sensory loss and allodynia and confirms a neuroanatomically consistent distribution
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A bedside examination using cotton wool, pinprick, warm and cold objects, and vibration. Painless; takes 15-30 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Neuropathic pain screening questionnaires (DN4, painDETECT, LANSS)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Supports identification of a neuropathic pain mechanism
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A short questionnaire combined with a brief bedside sensory exam; completed in the clinic in 10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms and localizes the central lesion (thalamic or brainstem stroke, MS plaque, tumor) and excludes new pathology
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes lying still in the scanner, sometimes with contrast. Result within 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies spinal cord injury level, MS plaques, syringomyelia, or tumor in patients with below-level or at-level pain
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes in the scanner. Result within 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Objectively documents the spinothalamic sensory deficit (impaired thermal and pain detection) characteristic of central pain
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A structured testing session applying calibrated thermal and mechanical stimuli; 45-60 minutes. Available at specialist pain centres.

diagnosticTests.test: Assessment for mood, sleep, and function
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies depression, anxiety, sleep disturbance, and disability that influence treatment and prognosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Validated questionnaires and clinical interview during the consultation.

treatmentOverview: Central pain syndrome is one of the most treatment-resistant pain conditions, and the realistic goal is meaningful pain reduction, improved sleep and function, and better quality of life rather than complete relief. Management is multidisciplinary, combining pharmacological therapy, physical rehabilitation, psychological support, and, in selected refractory cases, neuromodulation. First-line drugs, per NeuPSIG and Finnerup et al. 2015 evidence, are tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline 10-75 mg at night, or nortriptyline if anticholinergic effects are problematic), serotonin-noradrenaline reuptake inhibitors (duloxetine 30-120 mg daily), and the gabapentinoid anticonvulsants (gabapentin 900-3,600 mg/day in divided doses, or pregabalin 150-600 mg/day). These are titrated slowly to balance benefit against sedation, dizziness, and other side effects. Second-line options include lamotrigine (with particular evidence in central post-stroke pain), other anticonvulsants, and topical agents where there is a focal allodynic area. Opioids, including tramadol, have a limited and generally second- or third-line role because of modest efficacy in central pain and the risks of long-term use; if used, they require structured prescribing and monitoring. Spasticity-related and musculoskeletal pain components are treated separately (for example with antispasticity agents or physiotherapy). Non-pharmacological treatment is essential: graded physical and occupational therapy, transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation, mirror therapy and graded motor imagery in selected patients, sleep optimization, and psychological therapies such as cognitive behavioural therapy and acceptance and commitment therapy to reduce pain-related distress and disability. For refractory central pain, neuromodulation is considered at specialist centres: repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation of the motor cortex, motor cortex stimulation, deep brain stimulation, and intrathecal drug delivery have evidence in selected patients, although results are variable. Treatment of the underlying cause (for example MS disease-modifying therapy or surgical decompression of a syrinx) is pursued where applicable.

medicalTreatments.name: Amitriptyline 10-75 mg orally at night (tricyclic antidepressant)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line agent for central neuropathic pain. Started low and titrated slowly. Common side effects are sedation, dry mouth, constipation, and orthostatic dizziness; use cautiously in older adults and those with cardiac disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Number-needed-to-treat approximately 3-4 for 50% pain reduction across neuropathic pain (Finnerup Lancet Neurol 2015, PMID 25575710)

medicalTreatments.name: Duloxetine 30-120 mg orally daily (SNRI)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line serotonin-noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor, often better tolerated than tricyclics in older adults. Side effects include nausea, dry mouth, and insomnia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Number-needed-to-treat approximately 6 for 50% pain reduction in neuropathic pain; also treats coexisting depression

medicalTreatments.name: Pregabalin 150-600 mg/day orally in divided doses (gabapentinoid)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line anticonvulsant with predictable pharmacokinetics. Titrated over weeks. Side effects include dizziness, somnolence, weight gain, and peripheral edema; dose-reduce in renal impairment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Number-needed-to-treat approximately 7-8 for 50% pain reduction; evidence specifically in central post-stroke pain and spinal cord injury pain

medicalTreatments.name: Gabapentin 900-3,600 mg/day orally in divided doses (gabapentinoid)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line anticonvulsant alternative to pregabalin. Requires slower titration and more frequent dosing; dose-reduce in renal impairment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Number-needed-to-treat approximately 6-8 for 50% pain reduction in neuropathic pain

medicalTreatments.name: Lamotrigine 25-400 mg/day orally (anticonvulsant)
medicalTreatments.description: Second-line option with specific trial evidence in central post-stroke pain. Requires very slow titration to reduce the risk of serious rash (Stevens-Johnson syndrome).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest benefit in central post-stroke pain in randomized trials; reserved for patients intolerant of or unresponsive to first-line agents

medicalTreatments.name: Tramadol or other opioids (second/third line)
medicalTreatments.description: Limited role in central pain because of modest efficacy and long-term risks. If used, prescribed within a structured plan with clear review points, functional goals, and monitoring for tolerance and misuse.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest short-term analgesia in some patients; long-term benefit in central pain is not well established

medicalTreatments.name: Topical agents (lidocaine 5% patch, capsaicin) for focal allodynia
medicalTreatments.description: Used when there is a discrete area of cutaneous allodynia. Avoids systemic side effects. Limited in central pain because the painful area is often large and deep.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Useful adjunct for focal allodynic regions; not effective for diffuse central pain

surgicalOptions.name: Motor cortex stimulation
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical implantation of an epidural electrode over the motor cortex, used in specialist centres for refractory central post-stroke pain and other central pain. Pain relief is variable and not durable in all patients.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Meaningful pain relief in roughly 40-60% of carefully selected patients in case series; response is difficult to predict

surgicalOptions.name: Deep brain stimulation
surgicalOptions.description: Stimulation of the sensory thalamus or periaqueductal/periventricular grey matter for refractory central pain. Reserved for highly selected patients at experienced functional neurosurgery centres.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Variable; long-term meaningful relief in a minority of selected patients

surgicalOptions.name: Intrathecal drug delivery
surgicalOptions.description: An implanted pump delivering analgesics (such as ziconotide or opioid-based combinations) directly into the cerebrospinal fluid. Considered for refractory cases after multidisciplinary review.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptom improvement in selected refractory patients; requires ongoing specialist management

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical treatment of the underlying lesion
surgicalOptions.description: Decompression of a syringomyelia cavity, resection of a spinal or thalamic tumor, or correction of a tethered cord can reduce central pain when a treatable structural cause is identified.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Variable; addresses the cause rather than guaranteeing pain relief

alternativeTreatments.name: Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) of the motor cortex
alternativeTreatments.description: Non-invasive neuromodulation delivered in repeated sessions; has evidence for short-term pain reduction in central pain and is used as a less invasive option than implanted stimulation, and sometimes to predict response to motor cortex stimulation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mirror therapy and graded motor imagery
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured rehabilitation techniques that retrain disrupted central sensorimotor processing; evidence is strongest in complex regional pain syndrome and phantom limb pain but is applied selectively in central pain.

whySeeSpecialist: Central pain syndrome is complex, treatment-resistant, and frequently mismanaged as ordinary musculoskeletal pain. Neurology confirms the central lesion and excludes progressive pathology; a multidisciplinary pain medicine service coordinates pharmacological titration, rehabilitation, psychological therapy, and, where appropriate, neuromodulation. Early specialist referral improves function and reduces the risk of escalating opioid use.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a neurologist or pain medicine specialist working within a multidisciplinary chronic pain service that includes physiotherapy, occupational therapy, and clinical psychology. For refractory disease, seek a centre with experience in neuromodulation (rTMS, motor cortex stimulation, deep brain stimulation). Avoid clinics that rely solely on escalating opioid prescriptions.
hospitalCriteria: Neurology service with MRI access for lesion confirmation
Multidisciplinary pain medicine service with physiotherapy, occupational therapy, and psychology
Pharmacy and protocols for safe gabapentinoid and antidepressant titration
Access to neuromodulation (rTMS, motor cortex stimulation, deep brain stimulation) for refractory cases
Rehabilitation medicine input for stroke and spinal cord injury survivors
keyFacilities: Stroke and spinal cord injury rehabilitation units
Clinical psychology and pain-focused mental health services
preventionStrategies: Optimize stroke prevention (blood pressure control, anticoagulation for atrial fibrillation, statin therapy, smoking cessation) to reduce the population burden of central post-stroke pain.
Provide early, structured rehabilitation after stroke and spinal cord injury, which is associated with better pain and functional outcomes.
Screen stroke and spinal cord injury survivors proactively for emerging neuropathic pain so treatment can begin early.
Treat depression, anxiety, and sleep disturbance early, as these amplify central pain.
Use MS disease-modifying therapy to reduce relapse-related lesion accumulation that can drive central pain.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain a regular sleep schedule and good sleep hygiene, as poor sleep markedly worsens central pain.
Engage consistently in graded physical activity and prescribed rehabilitation rather than avoiding movement.
Use pacing strategies to balance activity and rest and avoid pain flares.
Apply warmth and avoid sudden cold exposure if cold is a personal trigger.
Learn and practice relaxation, mindfulness, or cognitive behavioural pain-management techniques.
Take prescribed neuropathic pain medications consistently rather than only when pain is severe.

recommended: Balanced anti-inflammatory diet pattern (vegetables, fruit, fish, whole grains, olive oil)
Adequate fiber and fluids to counter constipation from tricyclics and opioids
Regular meals to support energy and mood during rehabilitation
Adequate protein intake to support physical reconditioning
avoid: Excess alcohol, which interacts with neuropathic pain medications and worsens sleep
Excess caffeine late in the day, which fragments sleep
Highly processed, high-sugar foods that contribute to weight gain alongside gabapentinoids
Grapefruit juice if it interacts with the specific medications prescribed

exerciseGuidelines: Graded aerobic activity, range-of-motion work, and strengthening guided by a physiotherapist improve function and can modestly reduce pain. Activity is introduced gradually with pacing to avoid flares. Aquatic therapy is well tolerated by many patients with stroke or spinal cord injury. Complete avoidance of movement worsens deconditioning and pain.
dailyManagement: Take neuropathic pain medications at the same times each day, as prescribed.
Keep a brief pain and activity diary to identify triggers and track treatment response.
Follow a consistent sleep routine and a graded daily activity plan.
Use pacing to break tasks into manageable segments with planned rest.
Practice a daily relaxation or mindfulness routine.
Attend scheduled multidisciplinary follow-up and report side effects or worsening promptly.
prognosis: Central pain syndrome is typically chronic and persistent, and complete pain relief is uncommon. With optimized multidisciplinary treatment, a substantial proportion of patients achieve clinically meaningful pain reduction (commonly defined as a 30-50% decrease) along with improved sleep, mood, and function, but a significant minority remain refractory. Central post-stroke pain may improve over the first one to two years in some patients, while others have lifelong symptoms; spinal cord injury central pain tends to be persistent. Prognosis is better when pain is identified and treated early, when depression and sleep disturbance are addressed, when the patient remains engaged in rehabilitation, and when opioid escalation is avoided. The underlying neurological condition (stroke, spinal cord injury, MS) also shapes overall outcome and function. Neuromodulation offers meaningful relief to a subset of refractory patients but responses are variable and difficult to predict. Realistic goal-setting — focused on function and quality of life rather than zero pain — improves patient satisfaction and reduces the harm of repeated unsuccessful escalation of analgesics.
recoveryTimeline: Pharmacological treatments are titrated over weeks; a fair trial of any first-line agent at adequate dose typically takes 6-8 weeks. Some patients with central post-stroke pain experience gradual improvement over the first 1-2 years; spinal cord injury central pain is usually persistent. Multidisciplinary rehabilitation and psychological therapy produce gradual functional gains over months. Neuromodulation responses, when they occur, are assessed over weeks to months.
complications: Chronic disabling pain limiting participation in rehabilitation and daily activities.
Depression, anxiety, and elevated suicide risk associated with intractable pain.
Sleep disorder driven by constant pain.
Opioid-related harm — tolerance, dependence, and adverse effects — when escalation is pursued in place of multidisciplinary care.
Medication side effects: sedation, falls, cognitive slowing, weight gain, and anticholinergic effects, particularly in older adults.
Social isolation, loss of employment, and reduced quality of life.
Deconditioning and secondary musculoskeletal pain from activity avoidance.

supportResources.name: International Association for the Study of Pain (IASP)
supportResources.description: Global professional body publishing neuropathic pain taxonomy, education, and treatment guidance.

supportResources.name: American Stroke Association
supportResources.description: Patient resources on life after stroke, including chronic pain and rehabilitation.

supportResources.name: Christopher & Dana Reeve Foundation
supportResources.description: Support and information for people living with spinal cord injury, including pain management.

supportResources.name: National Multiple Sclerosis Society
supportResources.description: Patient resources on MS-related pain and symptom management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Fibromyalgia causes widespread pain, fatigue, and sensitization without a focal central nervous system lesion. Central pain syndrome has a documented central lesion (stroke, spinal cord injury, MS) and a neuroanatomically matching pain distribution with spinothalamic sensory loss.

confusedWithConditions.name: Neuropathic Pain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Neuropathic pain is the broad category; central pain syndrome is the specific subtype arising from a central nervous system lesion. Peripheral neuropathic pain (from nerve, root, or plexus injury) has a peripheral lesion and a peripheral-nerve distribution rather than a central lesion.

confusedWithConditions.name: Complex regional pain syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: CRPS follows a limb injury and features regional pain with autonomic, trophic, and motor changes in the affected limb, without a central nervous system lesion. Central pain syndrome arises from a brain or spinal cord lesion and its distribution maps to that lesion.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Pain Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic pain syndrome describes persistent pain with disproportionate disability and psychosocial features, often without a single identifiable lesion. Central pain syndrome is specifically attributable to a confirmed central nervous system lesion.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Pain Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Overlapping chronic pain population with shared multidisciplinary management principles.

relatedConditions.name: Neuropathic Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Central pain syndrome is a subtype of neuropathic pain and shares first-line pharmacotherapy.

relatedConditions.name: Multiple Sclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: A major cause of central pain through spinal and brainstem demyelination.

relatedConditions.name: Trigeminal Neuralgia
relatedConditions.relevance: A related central/neuropathic facial pain syndrome, frequently seen in multiple sclerosis.

relatedConditions.name: Phantom Limb Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Another neuropathic pain disorder driven by maladaptive central reorganization; shares rehabilitation strategies such as mirror therapy.

faqs.question: What is central pain syndrome?
faqs.answer: Central pain syndrome is chronic neuropathic pain caused by damage to the central nervous system itself — the brain, brainstem, or spinal cord — rather than peripheral nerves or tissues. It develops after stroke, spinal cord injury, multiple sclerosis, brain tumors, or traumatic brain injury.

faqs.question: What does central pain syndrome feel like?
faqs.answer: It is usually a constant burning, aching, or pressing pain, often with superimposed shooting pains. Many people have allodynia — pain from light touch, clothing, or temperature changes — over an area that is also numb to formal testing. The pain affects the body region matching the central lesion.

faqs.question: What causes central pain syndrome?
faqs.answer: It is caused by lesions of the central pain pathways. The most common cause is stroke (central post-stroke pain), followed by spinal cord injury, multiple sclerosis, brain or spinal cord tumors, syringomyelia, and traumatic brain injury. The shared mechanism is loss of inhibitory control and central sensitization.

faqs.question: How soon after a stroke does central pain develop?
faqs.answer: Central post-stroke pain often begins weeks to months after the stroke rather than immediately. Some patients develop it within days, but a delayed onset is characteristic and can make the connection to the original stroke less obvious.

faqs.question: Is central pain syndrome curable?
faqs.answer: Central pain syndrome is rarely cured. The realistic goal is meaningful pain reduction, better sleep, and improved function. Many patients achieve a 30-50% reduction in pain with multidisciplinary treatment, but a significant minority remain treatment-resistant.

faqs.question: How is central pain syndrome diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical: chronic neuropathic-type pain, a confirmed central nervous system lesion on MRI, and a pain distribution that matches that lesion. Sensory testing shows impaired pain and temperature perception in the painful region, often with allodynia. Screening tools like DN4 support a neuropathic mechanism.

faqs.question: What medications treat central pain syndrome?
faqs.answer: First-line medications are tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline), the SNRI duloxetine, and gabapentinoid anticonvulsants (pregabalin, gabapentin). Lamotrigine is a second-line option with specific evidence in central post-stroke pain. Opioids have a limited role because of modest efficacy and long-term risks.

faqs.question: Does central pain syndrome show up on an MRI?
faqs.answer: The pain itself is not visible on MRI, but the causative central lesion usually is — for example a thalamic or brainstem stroke, a multiple sclerosis plaque, a syrinx, or a tumor. MRI confirms the lesion and excludes new or progressive pathology.

faqs.question: What is Dejerine-Roussy syndrome?
faqs.answer: Dejerine-Roussy syndrome is the classic form of central post-stroke pain caused by a stroke in the thalamus. It produces burning hemibody pain with allodynia and abnormal sensation on the side of the body opposite the lesion. It is named after the neurologists who described it in 1906.

faqs.question: Can central pain syndrome get better over time?
faqs.answer: Some patients with central post-stroke pain improve over the first one to two years, while others have lifelong symptoms. Spinal cord injury central pain tends to be persistent. Early treatment, addressing sleep and mood, and staying engaged in rehabilitation improve the chances of meaningful improvement.

faqs.question: Why does light touch hurt in central pain syndrome?
faqs.answer: This is called allodynia. Damage to central pain pathways causes the nervous system to misinterpret normally harmless signals — light touch, clothing, a breeze, or cool air — as painful. It reflects loss of normal inhibitory control and central sensitization within the brain or spinal cord.

faqs.question: Is central pain syndrome the same as fibromyalgia?
faqs.answer: No. Both involve central sensitization, but fibromyalgia causes widespread pain without a focal central nervous system lesion. Central pain syndrome arises from a specific brain or spinal cord lesion and produces pain in the body region that lesion controls.

faqs.question: Can spinal cord injury cause central pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Up to two-thirds of people with spinal cord injury develop chronic pain, and much of it is central neuropathic pain. Below-level pain is often diffuse and burning and can be most severe in regions that are completely numb.

faqs.question: What non-drug treatments help central pain syndrome?
faqs.answer: Graded physical and occupational therapy, sleep optimization, transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation, mirror therapy or graded motor imagery in selected patients, and psychological therapies such as cognitive behavioural therapy all help reduce pain-related distress and improve function. These work best alongside medication.

faqs.question: Is brain stimulation used for central pain syndrome?
faqs.answer: Yes, for refractory cases at specialist centres. Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation is non-invasive; motor cortex stimulation and deep brain stimulation are surgical options. They provide meaningful relief to a subset of carefully selected patients, but responses are variable and hard to predict.

faqs.question: Do opioids work for central pain syndrome?
faqs.answer: Opioids have only a limited role. They provide modest short-term relief in some patients but long-term benefit in central pain is not well established, and risks of tolerance, dependence, and side effects are significant. They are second- or third-line and require structured prescribing and monitoring.

faqs.question: Can stress make central pain syndrome worse?
faqs.answer: Yes. Emotional stress, fatigue, poor sleep, and low mood all amplify central pain. This is why treatment of depression, anxiety, and sleep disturbance, along with relaxation and psychological techniques, is a core part of management rather than an optional extra.

faqs.question: Does central pain syndrome affect both sides of the body?
faqs.answer: Usually it affects the body region corresponding to the central lesion. A thalamic stroke typically causes pain on one half of the body; a spinal cord injury causes pain at and below the injury level. The distribution maps to the damaged central structure.

faqs.question: What kind of doctor treats central pain syndrome?
faqs.answer: Care is best delivered by a multidisciplinary team — a neurologist to confirm the lesion, a pain medicine specialist to coordinate treatment, plus physiotherapy, occupational therapy, and clinical psychology. Refractory cases may also involve functional neurosurgery for neuromodulation.

faqs.question: How long does it take for central pain medication to work?
faqs.answer: Neuropathic pain medications are titrated slowly over weeks to balance benefit and side effects. A fair trial of a first-line drug at an adequate dose usually takes 6-8 weeks before deciding whether it is helping or should be switched.

symptomKeywords: burning pain one side of body after stroke
pain from light touch numb area
constant aching pain spinal cord injury
cold makes pain worse stroke survivor
metaTitle: Central Pain Syndrome: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Central pain syndrome: chronic neuropathic pain after stroke, spinal cord injury, or MS. Symptoms, diagnosis, medications and neuromodulation reviewed.

sources.title: Klit H, Finnerup NB, Jensen TS. 2009. Central post-stroke pain: clinical characteristics, pathophysiology, and management. Lancet Neurology 8(9):857-68.
Finnerup NB, Attal N, Haroutounian S, et al. 2015. Pharmacotherapy for neuropathic pain in adults: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Lancet Neurology 14(2):162-73.
Siddall PJ, McClelland JM, Rutkowski SB, Cousins MJ. 2003. A longitudinal study of the prevalence and characteristics of pain in the first 5 years following spinal cord injury. Pain 103(3):249-257.
Watson JC, Sandroni P. 2016. Central neuropathic pain syndromes. Mayo Clinic Proceedings 91(3):372-85.
Treede RD, Jensen TS, Campbell JN, et al. 2008. Neuropathic pain: redefinition and a grading system for clinical and research purposes. Neurology 70(18):1630-5.

Lefaucheur JP, Aleman A, Baeken C, et al. 2020. Evidence-based guidelines on the therapeutic use of repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS): an update. Clinical Neurophysiology 131(2):474-528.
International Association for the Study of Pain. 2021. IASP terminology and classification of chronic pain.
Bowsher D. 1996. Central pain: clinical and physiological characteristics. Journal of Neurology, Neurosurgery & Psychiatry 61(1):62-9.
